notes: If this check fails then the scan was not run on a supported RHEL Linux 8 operating system.
Locking a user due to inappropriate encryption is not currently available in simp. A mechanism to address this rule will be implemented in a future release.

Users had to make a conscious decision to set "NOPASSWD" in sudoers on a running system. Automatically undoing these settings could negatively impact the system.

There is currently no mechanisme in the product to remove all occurrences of 'ALL' from sudoers. A mechanism to report, but not change, the existence of this statement in sudoers will be implemented in a future release.

There is currently no mechanism in simp to remove all occurrences of '!authenticate' from sudoers. A mechanism to report, but not change, the existence of this statement in sudoers will be implemented in a future release.

The product does not manage the /var/log mount because it is unsafe to change /var/log to a mountpoint on a running system. Since /var/log isn't being managed, nodev cannot be enforced.
The product does not manage the /var/log mount because it is unsafe to change /var/log to a mountpoint on a running system. Since /var/log isn't being managed, nosuid cannot be enforced.
The product does not manage the /var/log mount because it is unsafe to change /var/log to a mountpoint on a running system. Since /var/log isn't being managed, noexec cannot be enforced.

The conditions for this are met in /etc/profile.d/tmux_shell.sh. Some machines will not have tmux installed so there is extra logic to make the shell only trigger when tmux is actually installed. This, however, causes the rule to fail.
While it won't explicitly remove tmux from the list of shells, including the useradd class will remove any unapproved shells from /etc/shells, including tmux.
We do not currently have the ability to identify and remediate existing users who do not have this set appropriately, only new users.
We do not currently have the ability to identify and remediate existing users who do not have this set appropriately, only new users.

There is currently no way for the product to extrapolate which audit tools are installed on the system. Since there is no way to know which tools are in use, permissions for those tools won't be controlled by the product.

There is currently no mechanism to control the content of /etc/crypto-policies/back-ends/opensslcnf.config. One may be implemented in the future.
Remediating this rule requires user input for username and a password, the product does not currently support user input to provide this information.
Fips mode is enabled as requested by this rule, however, the scan still fails.

The product will set the mode of the files referenced in this rule to "700" and "600" rather then "750" and "640". This will lock down the files and keep them secure so only admins and ISSMs can access them.

The product will set pam to lock a user's account after 3 failed login attempts, however, there will also be some extra options in the pam sections this rule requests remediation for. Since the line won't exactly match the ordering and content the rule proposes, the check for it will fail.

The product will set pam to lock a user's account after 3 failed login attempts in a 15 minute period, however, there will also be some extra options in the pam sections this rule requests remediation for. Since the line won't exactly match the ordering and content the rule proposes, the check for it will fail.
Enabling gpgcheck globally is as close as we can get for this check, users may require access to private repos that might not have encryption set up appropriately.
